message:
docs: extend Gemini review to concurrency (rcu.rs) + flush beads

GEMINI_REVIEW_SUMMARY.md:

- Extends the earlier MVCC WAL + btrfs RAID review pass to also
  cover crates/ffs-mvcc/src/rcu.rs. The overview and conclusion
  are rewritten to mention the RcuMap concurrency layer alongside
  the WAL and btrfs surfaces.
- Adds a fourth finding (Nit): RcuMap::insert correctly checks
  the update_count % churn_threshold and emits a warn! log, but
  RcuMap::replace and RcuMap::clear bulk-replace the map and
  bump update_count without running the churn check. An
  application that hammers replace at high frequency will never
  see the churn warning metric fire. Suggested fix is to copy
  the churn-threshold logging block from insert into replace /
  clear so observability is consistent across all write paths.
- Closing paragraph now attests that the arc-swap structures in
  rcu.rs enforce unsafe_code = "forbid" and the lock-free read
  path is correct.

.beads/issues.jsonl:

- br sync --flush-only output for the rcu.rs review, the btrfs
  walker refactor, the ext4 dx_root validation, and the rustfmt +
  try_from sweep. Status transitions only, no new issues.

- Optional tracing hooks gated behind feature flag.

Acceptance:
- Metrics can be queried from ffs-core and printed by ffs-cli inspect.

 {"id":"bd-g1ue","title":"Harden ffs-dir test coverage with property-based and boundary tests","description":"## What
Expand ffs-dir from 49 tests to 70+ with property-based tests (proptest) and additional
boundary/corruption tests.

## Why
ffs-dir has the lowest test count (49) of any non-trivial crate. Directory operations are
safety-critical: a bug in add_entry/remove_entry can corrupt the entire directory structure.
The crate has proptest as a dev-dependency but no property-based tests yet.

## Current Coverage (49 tests)
- init_dir_block: 5 tests (basic, tail, sizes, inode numbers)
- add_entry: 10 tests (split, reuse, enospc, reject, max name, types)
- remove_entry: 7 tests (coalesce, first entry, nonexistent, overlap)
- htree: 8 tests (insert, find, remove, edge cases)
- compute_dx_hash: 3 tests (deterministic, seeds, names)
- Multi-operation: 3 tests (fill, cycle, alternating)
- Corruption detection: 3 tests (rec_len zero, misaligned, exceeds block)

## Gaps to Fill
1. Property-based tests (proptest):
   - proptest_add_remove_roundtrip: random names (1..255 bytes), random inodes,
     add then remove → block parseable, entry gone
   - proptest_add_never_exceeds_block: random sequence of adds → never writes past limit
   - proptest_init_parse_roundtrip: random self_ino, parent_ino, reserved_tail →
     init_dir_block → parse_dir_block → exactly . and .. with correct inodes
   - proptest_htree_insert_always_sorted: random hash sequence → entries always sorted

2. Boundary tests:
   - add_entry with exactly 0 bytes of slack (entry fits perfectly, no split needed)
   - remove_entry of last entry in block (rec_len extends to block end)
   - init_dir_block with reserved_tail = block_size - 24 (minimum viable)
   - add_entry with name at every alignment boundary (1,2,3,4,5,8,12,16 bytes)

3. Corruption resilience:
   - add_entry with rec_len pointing past block boundary
   - remove_entry with circular rec_len chain (entry points back to itself)
   - add_entry to block of all zeros (no valid first entry)

4. Stress tests:
   - Fill 4096-byte block completely, verify parse_dir_block recovers all entries
   - Add 100 entries, remove 50, add 50 more, verify final state

## Implementation Plan
Add to the existing #[cfg(test)] module in crates/ffs-dir/src/lib.rs.
proptest is already a dev-dependency of ffs-dir.

## Test Plan
- cargo test -p ffs-dir --lib → all pass, count >= 70
- proptest cases: 128 per property test
- No flakes in 3 consecutive runs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 4+ property-based tests added
- [ ] 5+ boundary tests added
- [ ] 3+ corruption resilience tests added
- [ ] 2+ stress tests added
- [ ] Total test count >= 70
- [ ] Zero test flakes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T16:18:00.522669212Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T21:42:36.913247920Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T21:42:36.912968691Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-g59h","title":"Honor BackpressureDecision::Throttle in FUSE write path","description":"FrankenFuse::should_shed currently only checks for BackpressureDecision::Shed, so Degraded/Critical throttle decisions are effectively ignored and writes proceed at full speed. Apply throttle behavior (delay) for Throttle decisions and add regression test coverage.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-22T18:51:33.860444149Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T18:52:28.420379182Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T18:52:28.420348394Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backpressure","correctness","fuse"],"comments":[{"id":288,"issue_id":"bd-g59h","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Implemented BackpressureDecision::Throttle handling in FrankenFuse::should_shed: degraded/critical throttle now sleeps for BACKPRESSURE_THROTTLE_DELAY and proceeds instead of being ignored. Added regression test should_shed_with_degraded_gate_throttles_without_shedding. Validation: cargo fmt --check; cargo test -p ffs-fuse should_shed_with_degraded_gate_throttles_without_shedding; cargo test -p ffs-fuse should_shed_with_emergency_gate_sheds_writes; cargo test -p ffs-fuse should_shed_with_normal_gate_proceeds_all; cargo check/clippy -p ffs-fuse --all-targets; cargo test -p ffs-fuse.","created_at":"2026-02-22T18:52:28Z"}]}

Parser reports xattr value extends past data boundary and OpenFs readback returns only inline A bytes instead of inline plus continuation bytes. Verify inline-data/xattr continuation layout and reader composition.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"EmeraldCitadel","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:00:43.501279528Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T01:18:53.234739776Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","ext4","xattr"]}
 {"id":"bd-plyn","title":"Bitmap CRC: cross-validate crc32c crate against software implementation","description":"Write a test that computes CRC32C of the same 4096-byte bitmap data using:
(a) the crc32c crate 0.6.8 (current)
(b) a manual software CRC32C implementation (polynomial 0x1EDC6F41, reflected)
(c) the crc32fast crate (if available) for a third opinion

Compare all three results. If they differ, the crc32c crate has a hardware-acceleration bug on this CPU. If they agree, the bug is in our bridge function or the data we're checksumming.

Test data: a real mkfs.ext4 bitmap block (or a synthetic 4096-byte buffer with known CRC).

ACCEPTANCE: A test that definitively proves whether the crc32c crate produces correct results for 4096-byte inputs on this platform.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:07:11.470459311Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:25:26.728288089Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:25:26.727814833Z","close_reason":"Root cause found: bitmap CRC was chaining group_number + using full block length. Fixed in commit 20d65f4.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["crc32c","ext4","investigation"]}
 {"id":"bd-pqpu","title":"Implement FIEMAP ioctl passthrough in ffs-fuse","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-31T03:54:37.576185576Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-31T03:54:42.588757333Z","closed_at":"2026-03-31T03:54:42.588475389Z","close_reason":"Implemented FIEMAP ioctl in ffs-fuse with FsOps::fiemap trait method, ext4 extent mapping, FUSE ioctl handler, and 2 unit tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-pv9l","title":"e2compr: implement double/triple indirect write and cluster freeing","description":"Extend the indirect write support to double-indirect (i_block[13]) and triple-indirect (i_block[14]) levels. This covers files up to the ext4 maximum.

Also implement cascading free: when all pointers in an indirect block become 0, free the block and propagate the zero to the parent level. This prevents block leaks when large compressed files are deleted or truncated.

DEPENDS ON: Single-indirect write support (bd-z15u).

ACCEPTANCE: Compressed write works for arbitrarily large files. Deleting a compressed file frees all indirect blocks.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:18.026009802Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:53:04.109030647Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:53:04.108567410Z","close_reason":"Added double-indirect (i_block[13]) and triple-indirect (i_block[14]) write support with lazy intermediate block allocation. Extracted ensure_indirect_block and ensure_pointer_in_block helpers. 490 tests pass. Commit 06a4288.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2compr","ext4","write_path"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-pv9l","depends_on_id":"bd-z15u","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:28.301559286Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-xvoe","title":"Investigate and fix ext4 bitmap CRC32C mismatch against real mkfs.ext4 images","description":"PROBLEM: The kernel_reference integration test ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel fails because our computed block bitmap checksum (via verify_block_bitmap_checksum -> block_bitmap_checksum_value -> ext4_bitmap_checksum) does not match the checksum stored by mkfs.ext4 1.47.2 on real images.

EVIDENCE: On a fresh 8MB ext4 image (mkfs.ext4 -L test -b 4096 -q -O ^has_journal), seed=0xba6605a8, our code computes 0xcd45611c but the stored checksum is 0xb8339b40. The ext4_chksum bridge function (!crc32c_append(!raw, data)) is verified correct for known test vectors (b'hello' produces the expected result). The GDT field offsets (bg_block_bitmap_csum_lo at 0x18, bg_block_bitmap_csum_hi at 0x38) match the kernel struct. The csum_seed derivation (from s_checksum_seed when CSUM_SEED is set) is verified correct.

POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSES:
(a) The crc32c crate 0.6.8 may have a hardware-acceleration edge case on this CPU (the test passes for small inputs but may diverge on 4096-byte inputs).
(b) There may be a subtle difference in how the kernel chains CRC32C for bitmap checksums vs how we do it (e.g., the kernel might zero some bits in the bitmap before checksumming, or exclude the last N bytes).
(c) The group descriptor checksum field offset may differ between mkfs.ext4 versions.
(d) There's a SECOND bitmap checksum function (compute_bitmap_checksum at line 1743) that uses csum_seed^group (XOR) instead of chaining — this function is wrong but verify_block_bitmap_checksum doesn't use it. However, it may have been copy-pasted from or to somewhere unexpected.

INVESTIGATION PLAN:
1. Add a test that creates a fresh ext4 image with mkfs.ext4, reads the raw bitmap bytes, and computes our CRC step-by-step, printing intermediate values.
2. Cross-validate by computing the same CRC using a second Rust CRC32C implementation (e.g., crc32c-hw or manual software CRC).
3. If the crate is correct, hexdump the raw GDT bytes and verify our parser reads the stored checksum from the right offset.
4. Check if mkfs.ext4 zeroes any bitmap bits before checksumming (e.g., unused tail bits beyond blocks_per_group).
5. Check the kernel source for ext4_block_bitmap_csum_set() to see if it modifies the bitmap before checksumming.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel test passes on a fresh mkfs.ext4 image.
- The root cause is documented in a code comment.
- If the crc32c crate has a bug, switch to an alternative or add a workaround.
- The wrong compute_bitmap_checksum function (XOR-based) is either removed or clearly marked as deprecated.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:10.024539600Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535625271Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535051315Z","close_reason":"Root cause: (1) group_number was chained into bitmap CRC but kernel doesn't do this, (2) checksum length was full block instead of clusters/inodes_per_group/8. Fixed in commits 20d65f4 and 7f4d6eb. Kernel reference test now passes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","crc32c","ext4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-xvoe","depends_on_id":"bd-gagz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:34.977699896Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-xvoe","depends_on_id":"bd-plyn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:32.052708381Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-xvoe","depends_on_id":"bd-sf6w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:33.521375134Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-xzn0","title":"Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)","description":"# Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)

## GOAL
Replace traditional free-space bitmaps with succinct data structures (rank/select bitmaps) that support O(1) rank and select queries while using near-optimal space.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 7.1: Succinct bitmaps (Jacobson 1989, Clark 1996) store bitmaps in n + o(n) bits while supporting:
- rank(i): count set bits in positions [0, i) — O(1)
- select(k): find position of k-th set bit — O(1)

For free-space bitmaps, this enables:
- O(1) "how many free blocks in range?" (rank)
- O(1) "find k-th free block" (select) — replaces linear scan for allocation

## DESIGN
- Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) + blocks (every 2^9 bits)
- Select via rank binary search or Vigna's select structure
- Space overhead: ~6.25% over raw bitmap (vs 0% for plain bitmap, but queries are O(n))
- Integration: replace ffs-ext4 block bitmap and inode bitmap

## APPLICABILITY
- Optimization for large filesystems where bitmap scans are expensive
- Most impactful when block groups are large or filesystem is >90% full
- Could also accelerate mballoc buddy allocator queries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select
- [ ] Space overhead < 10% over raw bitmap
- [ ] Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for block/inode bitmaps","acceptance_criteria":"Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select operations. Space overhead below 10% over raw bitmap. Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) plus blocks (every 2^9 bits). Drop-in replacement for block and inode bitmaps via BlockBitmap trait. Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap at 50%, 80%, 95% fullness. Benchmark: rank/select latency vs linear scan. Unit tests: rank correctness for all-zeros, all-ones, random patterns. Unit tests: select correctness for sparse and dense bitmaps. Unit tests: round-trip serialization/deserialization. Property test: rank(select(k)) == k for all valid k.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralThrush","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:30:45.958783809Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736127822Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736029688Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["datastructure","freespace","succinct"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:05:32.714682331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":202,"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

@@ -5,7 +5,7 @@
 
 ## Overview
 
-I performed a spontaneous exploratory code review of the MVCC WAL subsystem (`crates/ffs-mvcc/src/wal.rs`, `wal_writer.rs`, `wal_replay.rs`) and the `ffs-ondisk` btrfs RAID parser (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/btrfs.rs`), checking for undefined behavior, memory leaks, panics, and edge-case correctness.
+I performed a spontaneous exploratory code review of the MVCC WAL subsystem (`crates/ffs-mvcc/src/wal.rs`, `wal_writer.rs`, `wal_replay.rs`), the concurrency components (`crates/ffs-mvcc/src/rcu.rs`), and the `ffs-ondisk` btrfs RAID parser (`crates/ffs-ondisk/src/btrfs.rs`), checking for undefined behavior, memory leaks, panics, lock-free safety, and edge-case correctness.
 
 ## Findings
 
@@ -34,6 +34,11 @@ I performed a spontaneous exploratory code review of the MVCC WAL subsystem (`cr
 * **Root Cause:** In `verify_or_rollback_coalesced_write`, if `verify_written_record` fails, the code restores `self.write_pos` to `base_offset` and truncates the file. This is fully correct. However, `self.appends_since_sync` is not incremented until *after* this call. This means on failure, the pending sync count remains accurate, which is great.
 * **Suggested Fix:** No fix needed; explicitly noting this as highly robust code.
 
+### 4. `crates/ffs-mvcc/src/rcu.rs`
+* **Severity:** Nit
+* **Root Cause:** The `RcuMap` tracks updates using `update_count.fetch_add` and invokes a `warn!` log when the modulo of the count hits the configured `churn_threshold`. This is properly implemented in `RcuMap::insert`. However, `RcuMap::replace` and `RcuMap::clear` bulk-replace the map and increment `update_count` but do *not* check for the `churn_threshold`. If an application is repeatedly calling `replace` at high frequencies, the churn warning metric will fail to trigger.
+* **Suggested Fix:** Copy the churn threshold logging block from `insert` into `replace` and `clear` to ensure consistent observability across all write-path methods.
+
 ## Conclusion
 
-The WAL persistence and BTRFS RAID logical-to-physical mapping logic are exceptionally robust. I did not find any instances of unsafe `unwrap()` usage in production paths that lacked proper bounds analysis, nor did I identify any OOM vectors in WAL replay. The explicit bounds checking and early-rejection of huge allocations in `decode_commit` are well implemented.
+The WAL persistence, concurrency layers, and BTRFS RAID logical-to-physical mapping logic are exceptionally robust. I did not find any instances of unsafe `unwrap()` usage in production paths that lacked proper bounds analysis, nor did I identify any OOM vectors in WAL replay. The `arc-swap` structures in `rcu.rs` enforce `unsafe_code = "forbid"` and properly lock-free read access perfectly.
